elements.sent-2411-001: We study the problem of dynamically maintaining the connected components of an undirected graph subject to edge insertions and deletions.
elements.sent-2411-002: We give the first parallel algorithm for the problem which is work-efficient, supports batches of updates, runs in polylogarithmic depth, and uses only linear total space.
elements.sent-2411-003: The existing algorithms for the problem either use super-linear space, do not come with strong theoretical bounds, or are not parallel.
elements.sent-2411-004: On the empirical side, we provide the first implementation of the cluster forest algorithm, the first linear-space and poly-logarithmic update time algorithm for dynamic connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-005: The problem of dynamically maintaining the connected components of a graph is a critical subroutine that is often used when designing dynamic algorithms for other fundamental and practical problems, e.g., dynamic DBSCAN~\cite{khan2014dbscan,GT2017 , hierarchical clustering~\cite{monath2023online,Ruan2021 , approximate MST~\cite{connectit , among other problems.}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: It is also one of the most intensely studied dynamic graph problems, and has seen extensive algorithmic development over the past three decades~\cite{henzinger1995randomized, eppstein1997sparsification, holm2001poly, thorup1999decremental, thorup2000near, henzinger2001maintaining, wulff2013faster, kapron2013dynamic, rasumssen16faster, huang17dynconn, nanongkai2017dynamic, wulff2017fully .}
elements.sent-2411-007: In the fully-dynamic graph connectivity problem, the goal is to build a data structure that supports the following three operations on a dynamic undirected graph $G=(V,E)$ with a fixed set of vertices:.
elements.sent-2411-008: Despite its importance, dynamic connectivity has not yet been efficiently solved in practice, and marks a major gap in our understanding of how to bridge theory and practice in dynamic algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the special case of dynamic forest-connectivity, there are data structures such as {\em Euler Tour Trees (ETTs) ~\cite{henzinger1995randomized that are reasonably practical and have been adapted to support parallel updates~\cite{tseng2019batch .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, few implementations exist for the more complex case of general graphs.
elements.sent-2411-011: For general graphs, perhaps the best known dynamic connectivity data structure that provides good theoretical guarantees and has been implemented is due to Holm, de Lichtenberg, and Thorup~\cite{holm2001poly , who developed a dynamic connectivity algorithm that performs updates in $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized time and requires $O(n\log n + m)$ space, for a graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Their algorithm is based on $O(\log n)$ layers of dynamic forest-connectivity data structures; we refer to their idea as the {\em HDT algorithm .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, existing implementations of the HDT algorithm suffer from high overheads in space and time, limiting their practical applicability.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, we found that to run on a 1.2 billion edge graph, an optimized dynamic connectivity implementation based on the HDT algorithm requires up to 360 billion bytes---over $70$ times more than what it takes to store the graph using a simple (static) representation.
elements.sent-2411-015: The key limitation of implementations based on the HDT algorithm is that the connectivity information is stored redundantly in a logarithmic number of forest-connectivity data structures across different layers.
elements.sent-2411-016: At a high level, the HDT algorithm maintains a spanning forest $F$ and hierarchy of nested edge subsets $F_1 \subseteq F_2 \subseteq \ldots F_k = F$.
elements.sent-2411-017: With this representation, \revision{the vertices of $G$ are present in $k$ trees , which results in a space usage of $O(n\log n)$ across all of the trees.}
elements.sent-2411-018: On the theoretical side, the space usage was improved to linear by an elegant {\em cluster forest algorithm (CF algorithm) that is inspired by the HDT algorithm~\cite{thorup2000near .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The key idea in the CF algorithm is to store a single forest of trees (called the {\em cluster forest ) that implicitly represents the connectivity information stored in the nested $O(\log n)$ layers of the HDT algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In its basic version, the CF algorithm achieves similar update times to HDT, while improving the space bound to $O(n+m)$.
elements.sent-2411-021: This was the first algorithm that solved dynamic graph connectivity in poly-logarithmic update time and linear space.
elements.sent-2411-022: The CF algorithm was later simplified and optimized by Wulff-Nilsen~\cite{wulff2013faster .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{figures/dyn-conn-intro-figure.pdf} \caption{ {\bf The core data structures used by the cluster forest (CF) and HDT algorithms.} The input graph is shown in (a). The cluster forest is given in (b), and represents the nested hierarchy of connected components. The leaves (level 0 nodes) are the original vertices. The internal nodes (level $> 0$) are colored using different colors per-level and are the nested components. (c) shows the cluster graph of the level 2 component $F$. The nodes in the cluster graph are the children of $F$, and the edges in the cluster graph are the level $2$ edges that are incident to vertices that $F$ contains (the level $2$ edges go between level $1$ components). Lastly, (d) shows the same component hierarchy as (b), but as stored by the HDT algorithm. The HDT algorithm stores a {\em separate} Euler Tour Tree (ETT) for {\em every} component in the hierarchy. Each tree edge is stored twice (illustrated as smaller white circles), once per direction.} \label{fig:introfig}
elements.sent-2411-023: Given the theoretical advantages of the CF algorithm, an important question is: {\bf is the CF algorithm practical? For example, does it yield improved space-efficiency or faster runtime in practice relative to existing dynamic connectivity implementations? This question is highly non-trivial due to the complexity of implementing and optimizing the CF algorithm, which uses a significant amount of indirection and requires performing sophisticated tree traversals and amortization to obtain its update bounds.}
elements.sent-2411-024: A second important question is: {\bf can the CF algorithm be efficiently parallelized? In particular, can we ensure that each update is processed with low depth (longest chain of sequentially dependent instructions) even in the worst-case? Furthermore, can we also make the algorithm work efficiently in the parallel batch-dynamic setting? We note that the batch-dynamic setting, in which updates come in batches of arbitrary size, is the standard modern setting for parallelizing dynamic algorithms~\cite{acar2019parallel, hanauer2022recent, pandey2021terrace, liu2022parallel, besta2019practice, gabert2021shared, dhulipala2020parallel .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Ideally we would like to parallelize the algorithm without sacrificing space-efficiency or work-efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-026: That is, each batch of updates should be performed with low depth, and work (total number of operations) and space matching that of the sequential CF algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-027: We note that while the HDT algorithm was recently shown to be amenable to an efficient batch-dynamic algorithm~\cite{acar2019parallel , it is not space-efficient.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this paper, we carefully study the CF approach in theory and practice to answer these open questions.
elements.sent-2411-029: On the theoretical side, we extend the CF algorithm and show how to achieve low depth.
elements.sent-2411-030: Specifically, we introduce a new invariant (the {\em blocked invariant ), which provides important additional structure that we exploit.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Using our new invariant and our approach to maintaining it, we obtain the first space-efficient and work-efficient parallel algorithm that has poly-logarithmic depth.
elements.sent-2411-032: On the empirical side, we perform the first experimental study of the CF approach in the sequential setting.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the next two sections, we formalize the data structures, and present a technical overview of our results.

elements.sent-2411-068: The graph search, which needs to discover the connected component of a graph undergoing changes, seems extremely difficult to parallelize work-efficiently using the existing CF algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-069: The main issue is that the graph in the cluster forest at this level can potentially have very high diameter; in fact, the diameter can be as high as $\Theta(n)$, making a work-efficient parallelization of this process very challenging unless we are willing to sacrifice depth.
elements.sent-2411-070: We solve this problem by introducing a new invariant called the {\em blocked invariant that ensures that the cluster graph stored at every internal node in the cluster forest is {\em guaranteed to have constant diameter (in fact the diameter is always at most $2$).}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The key idea of the invariant is simple to state: we ensure that every cluster is incident to at least one edge that cannot be further pushed down to a lower level without violating the size invariant.
elements.sent-2411-072: We prove that this property implies that the cluster graph at each node is guaranteed to have diameter at most $2$.
elements.sent-2411-073: As a result, during replacement search any parallel graph procedure (e.g., parallel breadth-first search) will suffice, and help us obtain low depth, since the diameter is low.
elements.sent-2411-074: However, maintaining this property dynamically turns out to be very tricky even in the sequential setting; our main algorithmic contributions are novel sequential and parallel algorithms to maintain the invariant under (batch-) dynamic updates.
elements.sent-2411-075: Overall, our new parallel batch-dynamic algorithm can perform insertions and deletions with $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized work per edge update and $O(\log^3 n)$ depth per batch.

elements.sent-2411-078: Our experiments show that our new implementations of the CF approach are significantly more space-efficient and process updates faster than existing state-of-the-art dynamic connectivity implementations.
elements.sent-2411-079: For example, across a diverse set of graph inputs, our CF implementations achieve up to 19.7$\times$ lower space usage compared with a carefully designed implementation of HDT, and up to 6.2$\times$ faster updates.

elements.sent-2411-342: Our goals in this part of the paper are to understand (1) whether the cluster forest algorithm is practical and yields good query and update performance; (2) whether the theoretical space improvements provided by the cluster forest algorithm translate into meaningful space improvements in practice; and (3) whether the algorithm can scale to large graphs and work well across a variety of different graph types (both real-world and synthetic).
elements.sent-2411-343: Since there have been no prior implementations of the cluster forest approach, we focus our study on sequential implementations in order to carefully study different design choices in the algorithm and carefully measure the impact of these choices on runtime and query performance.
elements.sent-2411-344: In this section, we demonstrate that a carefully optimized implementation of the cluster forest algorithm can achieve all three of these goals, and that the cluster forest approach may be the algorithm of choice when both theoretical guarantees and practical performance are required.. \revision{.}
elements.sent-2411-345: A major bottleneck when implementing the algorithm is the cost of traversing the cluster-forest hierarchy, a step that occurs in nearly all aspects of the algorithm (e.g., replacement edge search, fetching a level $i$ edge, or pushing an edge from level $i$ to $i-1$).
elements.sent-2411-346: Although the hierarchy has depth $O(\log n)$, traversals can still be costly as they encounter both cluster forest nodes and local tree nodes, and thus every traversal involves significant pointer jumping.
elements.sent-2411-347: To address this issue, we designed our implementation to reduce the cost of and eliminate tree traversals whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-348: Due to space constraints, we provide more a detailed description and discussion of our optimizations in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-349: Our first optimization is called \defn{flattened local trees .}
elements.sent-2411-350: As the name suggests, we flatten the local tree structure into an array that store the roots of the rank trees in increasing order of rank instead of combining the rank trees into a binary tree (see Section~\ref{sec:seqcf for local and rank tree definitions).}
elements.sent-2411-351: Since there are at most $\log_2 n$ rank trees, this array approach does not sacrifice the time complexity of any local tree operation and improves locality.
elements.sent-2411-352: Additionally, we do not combine rank trees nodes unless there are more than $\log_2 n$ of them.
elements.sent-2411-353: This means that in many cases where a node in the cluster forest has few children, the algorithm can avoid a large amount of indirection in traversing the local trees and rank trees.
elements.sent-2411-354: Our next optimization is called \defn{lowest common ancestor (LCA) insertion .}
elements.sent-2411-355: The optimization is to insert an edge at the level of the lowest level node in the cluster forest that contains both endpoints of the edge.
elements.sent-2411-356: A faithful implementation of the cluster forest algorithm is to simply perform \defn{root insertion , i.e., every non-tree edge insertion is simply placed at the root of its tree.}
elements.sent-2411-357: The LCA optimization trades off extra time spent during an insertion to find the LCA to distribute the edges better across the levels to achieve faster deletions, since fewer edges need to be searched, thus lowering the amount of traversals.
elements.sent-2411-358: The performance overhead of performing LCA insertion is negligible compared to root insertion---on average LCA insertion is $1.06\times$ faster on the graphs we evaluated when comparing total insertion time.
elements.sent-2411-359: However, LCA insertion makes a huge difference for speeding up deletions---on average, it speeds up total deletion time by $1.5\times$ on average over root insertion across all of our graphs.
elements.sent-2411-360: We note that the LCA optimization is somewhat unique to the cluster forest algorithm, since finding the LCA can be done in $O(\log n)$ time using the cluster forest representation; applying a similar optimization to HDT seems more complex as it requires quickly finding the lowest level where the endpoints of an edge are connected, which naively takes $O(\log^2 n)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-361: When evaluating an early version of our algorithm, we found that on dense graphs, our implementation was slower than HDT, even when using the LCA optimization.
elements.sent-2411-362: The reason for HDTs speed is that $\geq m-n+1$ edges are {\em non-tree edges , and therefore, many deletions in dense graphs target non-tree edges.}
elements.sent-2411-363: HDT benefits from this fact, since a non-tree edge deletion simply checks a hash-table storing whether an edge is tree or non-tree in $O(1)$ time, and updates bitmaps after deleting the edge.
elements.sent-2411-364: To achieve similar benefits in the cluster forest algorithm, we introduce a {\bf non-tree edge tracking optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-365: Due to space constraints we give a full description of the optimization in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-366: In a nutshell, the idea of the optimization is to carefully mark edges as either tree or non-tree---as in HDT, non-tree edges can simply be deleted without affecting the connectivity information.
elements.sent-2411-367: On dense graphs, the non-tree edge tracking optimization yields a significant speedup---for example, on Orkut, we observed a speedup of $1.87\times$ after implementing our non-tree edge tracking optimization due to 87\% of the deletions being detected as non-tree edges.
elements.sent-2411-368: Compared to HDT, which detects 89\% of the deletions as non-tree edges our optimization shows that our CF implementation can almost completely match the HDT implementation's ability to avoid unnecessary work during edge deletions.
elements.sent-2411-369: All of the experiments presented in this paper were run on a machine with 4 $\times$ 2.1 GHz Intel Xeon(R) Platinum 8160 CPUs (each with 33MiB L3 cache) and 1.5TB of main memory.
elements.sent-2411-370: We refer to our implementations with and without the LCA insertion optimization as \defn{CF-LCA and \defn{CF-Root , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-371: We compare against a faithful implementation of the original HDT algorithm~\cite{holm2001poly written in C++ also optimized using set data structures from Abseil.}
elements.sent-2411-373: We also compare against D-Tree~\cite{chen2022spanning , a recently published data structure for dynamic connectivity that is written in Python.}
elements.sent-2411-374: We note that since D-Tree is implemented in Python comparing its running time and memory usage with other implementations written in C++ may be unfair; however, since D-tree is a recent linear-space dynamic connectivity algorithm, we include it for completeness.
elements.sent-2411-375: To generate dynamic updates, we generate a random permutation of all of the edges in the graph, \revision{and insert all of the edges in this order.}
elements.sent-2411-376: Then we generate another random permutation of the edges \revision{and delete all of the edges in this order.}
elements.sent-2411-377: We break these random permutations into 10 \defn{stages of inserting $|E|/10$ edges per-stage and then 10 stages of deleting $|E|/10$ edges.}
elements.sent-2411-378: We use stages to understand how memory usage, and the insert and delete speeds change over time (e.g., as the graph grows more dense, and then more sparse).

elements.sent-2411-380: Our goal is to determine (1) whether memory usage is a limiting factor in the ability of HDT-based implementations to scale to extremely large graphs, (2) whether implementations based on the CF algorithm using linear space can overcome this obstacle, and (3) whether the memory usage of implementations based on the CF algorithm can perform better than existing state-of-the-art dynamic connectivity implementations using linear space.
elements.sent-2411-381: Our results affirmatively answer all three of these questions.
elements.sent-2411-382: We report the raw (unnormalized) numbers for peak memory usage in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-383: Our main finding is that our CF implementations consistently require significantly less memory than HDT.
elements.sent-2411-384: This is because with root insertion there are slightly fewer nodes in the cluster forest.
elements.sent-2411-385: On the sparsest graph we tested (Grid) the CF algorithm uses 294 bytes per edge while HDT uses 5,809 bytes per edge.
elements.sent-2411-386: For sparse graphs (e.g. USA Roads, StackOverflow, Grid) where $m$ is closer to $n$, the $O(n+m)$ space usage of CF should beat the $O(n\log n + m)$ space usage of HDT, and this asymptotic difference can be clearly seen in the results.
elements.sent-2411-387: Interestingly, even for dense graphs (e.g. ENWiki, Orkut, Twitter) where $m > n \log_2 n$, CF still uses significantly less memory than HDT.
elements.sent-2411-388: This is because in dynamic connectivity algorithms edges can be stored very space efficiently ($\approx 10$ bytes per edge) while the space overhead of the tree data structures used in virtually all dynamic connectivity algorithms scales heavily with the number of vertices (at least a hundred bytes per vertex per tree).
elements.sent-2411-389: This supports the conclusion that on real-world graphs (which are typically quite sparse) dynamic connectivity algorithms that require storing a large number of trees over the vertices (like HDT) prevent scaling to very large graphs.
elements.sent-2411-390: The extra $\log n$ factor on the space usage, which may easily be overlooked in theory, {\em has a massive impact in practice .}
elements.sent-2411-391: Although it is implemented in Python, D-Tree still uses significantly less memory than HDT in all of our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-392: This is again indicative of the large impact of using data structures that have linear total space in practice.

elements.sent-2411-395: Our results show that our CF implementations can achieve \revision{significantly better performance than HDT for both insertions and deletions for all all graphs in our experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-396: The improvements in update speed for the CF implementations can be attributed to the optimizations we described in Section~\ref{sec:implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-397: For example, for non-tree edge insertions, both algorithms must simply add the edge to the edge set, and then traverse up to update $O(\log n)$ bitmaps; the flattened local tree optimization decreases the cost of such traversals in the CF implementation resulting in faster insertions.
elements.sent-2411-398: For non-tree edge deletions, the non-tree edge tracking optimization enables our CF implementation to match the performance of HDT.
elements.sent-2411-399: With the optimization, both algorithms simply check the hash-table once, delete the edges from the edge set, and traverse up to update the bitmaps.
elements.sent-2411-400: The flattened local tree optimization once again allows the CF implementations to outperform the HDT implementation due to the lower cost of traversing the hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-401: This is because while the LCA optimization slows down insertions slightly, deletions become much faster due to fewer edges needing to be inspected.
elements.sent-2411-402: However, CF-Root benefits from being able to find replacement edges very quickly in dense, well connected graphs like Twitter.
elements.sent-2411-403: We also compared our CF implementations with D-Tree, which sacrifices worst-case guarantees but obtains linear space usage.
elements.sent-2411-404: We note that updates for D-Tree can take much longer on certain graphs; e.g., D-Tree is $173\times$ slower than CF-LCA on the Household Lines graph.
elements.sent-2411-405: Since the algorithm does not have worst-case guarantees, we believe it is unlikely to be much faster on these bad cases even if it was implemented in a different language.
elements.sent-2411-406: For example, we observe a strong positive correlation between density and improved running time for HDT; e.g., despite Twitter being one of the largest graphs we evaluate on, its high density make HDT perform significantly better.
elements.sent-2411-407: We report the raw (unnormalized) numbers for query time in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-408: Our main finding is that our CF implementations do not sacrifice any performance in terms of query speed compared to HDT.
elements.sent-2411-409: This is expected, because in both types of algorithms, queries are answered by traversing to the root of the components of the two vertices which requires $O(\log n)$ memory reads and a single equality check.
elements.sent-2411-410: We believe this is further indicative that the height of the cluster forest hierarchy is generally lower than that of the top level spanning forest data structure in HDT due to the flattened local tree optimization.
elements.sent-2411-411: First, on the theoretical side, we give the first parallel batch-dynamic algorithm for maintaining the connected components of an undirected graph that is work-efficient, runs in poly-logarithmic depth, and only uses linear total space.
elements.sent-2411-412: Second, we give the first empirical study of the cluster forest algorithm in the sequential setting, introduce new optimizations to improve its practicality, and demonstrate its superior performance and space-efficiency in practice.
elements.sent-2411-413: Taken together, our results indicate that the CF algorithm is an excellent candidate for a practically scalable dynamic connected components algorithm with good theoretical guarantees.

elements.sent-2411-469: The following lemmas are useful for analyzing the work bounds of our parallel algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-470: Proofs are provided by Acar et al.~\cite{acar2019parallel .}
elements.sent-2411-471: The interface is defined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-472: BatchInsert$(c_1,...,c_k)$ takes a list of $k$ new leaf nodes and adds them to the local tree.
elements.sent-2411-473: BatchDelete$(c_1,...,c_k)$ takes a list of $k$ leaf nodes in $T$ and removes them from the local tree.
elements.sent-2411-474: Smallest$(C)$ returns the smallest element in the local tree.
elements.sent-2411-475: GetMaximalPrefix$(s)$ returns a maximal set of the elements in the local tree such that their total size is less than or equal to $s$ and no element excluded from the set has size less than any included element (e.g. the set returned is a prefix of the elements sorted by increasing size).
elements.sent-2411-476: GetMaximalPrefixOfSubset$(C,s)$ returns a maximal set of the elements in the subset $C$ of the elements in the local tree such that their total size is less than or equal to $s$ and no element excluded from the set has size less than any included element.
elements.sent-2411-477: Mark$(c)$ marks an element to indicate something about it.
elements.sent-2411-478: GetUnmarkedElement$()$ return an element in $T$ that has not been marked.
elements.sent-2411-479: The tree is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-480: The elements are divided into size classes where two elements $x$ and $y$ are in the same size class if $\lfloor \log n(x) \rfloor = \lfloor \log n(y) \rfloor$.
elements.sent-2411-481: For each size class, a weight-balanced tree is maintained for all of the elements in that class.
elements.sent-2411-482: The weight of any leaf in the weight-balanced tree is just $1$.
elements.sent-2411-483: The key for each element in the weight-balanced tree is its size.
elements.sent-2411-484: The weight-balanced trees are also augmented with the total size of elements in a subtree.
elements.sent-2411-485: Then the usual rank trees and local tree are applied, treating each weight-balanced tree $t$ as a leaf with rank $\lfloor \log n(t) \rfloor$ where $n(t)$ is the total size of all the elements in $t$.
elements.sent-2411-486: The depth of any leaf node in a cluster forest using modified local trees is $O(\log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-487: At every node in the weight-balanced trees, the total size in the left and right subtree is still off by at most a constant factor because every element in the same weight class has at most twice the size of any other element.
elements.sent-2411-488: Then for the local trees the same argument as before applies.
elements.sent-2411-489: For a child $v$ of a node $u$ in the cluster forest, the depth of $v$ in the local tree is at most $\log(n(u)/n(v)) + 1$.
elements.sent-2411-490: Since there are $\log n$ levels in the cluster forest, a telescoping sums argument implies that any leaf of the local tree forest has depth $O(\log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-491: An insertion or deletion into a modified local tree works as follows.
elements.sent-2411-492: First insert or the delete the element from the weight-balanced tree for its size class.
elements.sent-2411-493: Then the total size in its tree will have changed.
elements.sent-2411-494: Since each weight-balanced tree is treated as a leaf in the rank trees and local tree, just delete and re-insert the leaf for that tree with its new total size as its rank.
elements.sent-2411-495: For batch insertion or deletion first semisort the batch by weight class.
elements.sent-2411-496: Then for each class, do a batch insertion or deletion into the weight-balanced tree for that class.
elements.sent-2411-497: Finally, the entire rank trees and local tree are removed and rebuilt from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-498: Inserting or deleting a batch of $k$ elements into a modified local tree can be done in $O(\log n)$ depth and $O(k\log(1+n/k))$ work where $n$ is the total number of elements in tree.

elements.sent-2411-785: Theoretically, these edge sets should be stored in a nested map where the key of the outer map is the level, and the value is a set which contains all edges at this level.

elements.sent-2411-787: Since the algorithm mostly operates on the {\em inner edge set , lookups on the outer map can become a bottleneck.}
elements.sent-2411-788: Therefore, we replace the outer map with a vector.
elements.sent-2411-789: Importantly, we can implement this change without sacrificing the time or space complexity of the data structure.
elements.sent-2411-790: Our approach replaces the outer map with a vector as follows: the vector stores a collection of pairs in which the first element is the level, and the second element is a pointer to the edge set that stores the edges corresponding to that level.
elements.sent-2411-791: To quickly locate the index of the level $i$ edge set, we can simply take the bitmap for this leaf, zero-out all bits corresponding to levels larger than $i$ (this can be done in $O(1)$ time by performing a bit-shift), and finally counting the number of set bits in this modified bitmask (e.g., using popcount).
elements.sent-2411-792: This entire operation can be done in $O(1)$ time using bit-operations on words, and is extremely fast, and yields the edge set corresponding to the level $i$ edges.
elements.sent-2411-793: We note that operations on the collection of edge sets that introduce a new edge set or deleting an empty edge set still require $O(\log n)$ time, matching the theoretical complexity of the naive solution.
elements.sent-2411-794: However, many operations that work solely within already-existing edge sets can be sped up significantly in practice, although this does not yield an asymptotic improvement in the running time.
elements.sent-2411-795: Both algorithms store this spanning forest in an Euler Tour Tree which is used to answer queries for whether two vertices are connected.

elements.sent-2411-797: Unfortunately in the cluster forest data structure, there is no clear notion of a tree or non-tree edge, and obtaining a similar speedup as in the HDT/HK algorithms by avoiding work when processing non-tree edge deletions is not at all obvious.
elements.sent-2411-798: One kind of edge in the cluster forest that can be deleted cheaply is a \defn{self-loop edge , a level $i$ edge such that the level $(i-1)$ cluster representatives of both $u$ and $v$ are identical.}
elements.sent-2411-799: However, testing whether an edge is a self-loop edge requires $O(\log n)$ time in order to traverse the cluster forest and find the level $(i-1)$ cluster representatives of the level $i$ edge.
elements.sent-2411-800: However, in the HDT algorithm checking if an edge is a non-tree edge takes $O(1)$ time since this information is stored in a hash-table.
elements.sent-2411-801: This fact makes HDT much more practical on dense graphs where most of the deletions target non-tree edges.
elements.sent-2411-802: Before introducing our optimization, it is instructive to think about a simplistic approach to identify tree vs. non-tree edges.
elements.sent-2411-803: Suppose that we mark the first insertion that connects two components as a tree edge, and all subsequent insertions that don't affect connectivity as non-tree edges.
elements.sent-2411-804: Now, suppose we delete some other tree edge and perform a replacement edge search.
elements.sent-2411-805: During the replacement edge search, the algorithm may end up pushing down some of the non-tree edges that are parallel to a tree edge (thus merging the clusters that this tree edge goes between), while leaving this tree edge at its original level.
elements.sent-2411-806: Subsequently, one of these pushed-down non-tree edges could get deleted, but since this edge is marked as a non-tree edge, we would not perform a replacement search, and could leave the cluster-graph at a lower level disconnected (also note that the tree edge becomes a self-loop at its unmodified level, and the notion of doing a ``replacement search'' is ill defined since both level $(i-1)$ representatives of the deleted edge are actually the same).
elements.sent-2411-807: This example illustrates that any tree/non-tree marking strategy should be extremely careful to ensure that each cluster graph at every level $i$ is {\em connected .}
elements.sent-2411-808: Our optimization based on this idea is called \defn{non-tree edge tracking , and works as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-809: When inserting an edge $(u,v)$ that connects two different components, we mark this edge as a tree edge.
elements.sent-2411-810: Otherwise, if the edge goes within the same component, we mark this edge as a non-tree edge.
elements.sent-2411-811: This part of our optimization is extremely natural.
elements.sent-2411-812: What is significantly more interesting is how to update tree/non-tree edge designations during a deletion and replacement edge search.
elements.sent-2411-813: If an edge $e=(u,v)$ is deleted and $e$ is marked as non-tree, we simply delete it, and update the bitmaps for $u$ and $v$.
elements.sent-2411-814: Otherwise, if $e=(u,v)$ is a level $i$ tree edge, we perform replacement edge search from the level $(i-1)$ representatives $C_u$ and $C_v$.
elements.sent-2411-815: When performing the replacement edge search, for each level $(i-1)$ cluster found, we mark the first edge that reaches that cluster as a tree edge.
elements.sent-2411-816: We note that this marking occurs in both of the searches that we perform---both the search out of $C_u$ and the search out of $C_v$.
elements.sent-2411-817: A reader may wonder whether the amount of edges marked as tree is much larger than necessary.
elements.sent-2411-818: Although we are not sure whether this approach is tight in a theoretical sense (i.e., whether we may be over-marking edges as tree), the important property of our optimization is that it is {\em correct , and that it is also highly effective in practice and empirically does not seem to overmark too many edges unnecessarily as tree edges (as we mentioned in the main body of the paper, there is a few percentage difference in the number of non-tree edges we detect compared with HDT).}

elements.sent-2411-834: The replacement search procedure maintains two separate two-level queues for $C_u$ and $C_v$'s searches, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-835: During a search, we first check the leaf queue.
elements.sent-2411-836: If the leaf-queue is non-empty, we take the first leaf, extract a level $i$ edge from this leaf, and traverse it to find the neighboring leaf and corresponding level $(i-1)$ cluster.
elements.sent-2411-837: If the neighboring leaf and cluster are not already in their respective queues, we insert them into the appropriate queue.
elements.sent-2411-838: To avoid extracting the same edge again, we simply increment the iterator of this leaf's edge set; if all level $i$ edges incident to this leaf are extracted, we update this leaf's bitmaps and move on.
elements.sent-2411-839: When the search runs out of leaves in the leaf queue, it pops the next level $(i-1)$ cluster from the cluster queue, and uses the bitmaps to find the next unvisited leaf incident to this cluster, adding this leaf to the leaf queue.
elements.sent-2411-840: If both queues become empty for a search, then the replacement search at this level is done (and we failed to find a replacement edge).
elements.sent-2411-841: The effect of our optimization is to save a substantial amount of time performing downward traversals in the cluster forest to find nodes containing level $i$ edges, since we explicitly maintain a queue of them.
